concepts.title: Primitive or simple cubic (PC/SC) unit cell
concepts.conceptBody:
Primitive Or Simple Cubic (PC/SC) Unit Cell

In a primitive cubic unit cell, all the eight corners of the cube are occupied by atoms and they are not present anywhere else in the cube. If all the eight atoms are of same type, the unit cell is called ideal primitive and if they are not same, it is referred as pseudo primitive or primitive-like.

Figure (a) shows an ideal primitive cubic unit cell.

In this layer, each atom is in contact with its immediate neighbours. If the centres of these four atoms are joined, a face of the unit cell can be generated. Now, place a layer of four other atoms over this layer in such a way that the atoms of the second layer eclipse the atoms of the first layer and join the centre of all the eight atoms to get a primitive cubic arrangement.

The volume of a cubic unit cell is not completely occupied by atoms. The ratio of volume occupied by the effective atoms to the volume of the unit cell is called packing fraction (PF). The fraction of volume of a unit cell, that is empty is called void fraction (VF). Thus,

$\text{Packing fraction }=\frac{\text{ Volume occupied by the effective atoms in a unit cell }}{\text{ Volume of the unit cell }\left( {{\text{a}}^{3}} \right)}$

$\text{ and void fraction }=(1-\text{ Packing fraction })\qquad $

$\therefore \text{PF }=\frac{1\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{r}}^{3}}}{{{(2\text{r})}^{3}}}=\frac{\pi }{6}=0.523$

Thus, the structure is relatively open since only $52.3%$ of the total volume is occupied by the atoms. The remainder $47.7%$ of the total volume is empty space or void volume. In general, the packing fraction is independent of the radius of atom and edge length of the unit cell and depends only on the nature of packing. This type of structure is exhibited by polonium, Po.

concepts.title: Body centered cubic (BCC) unit cell
concepts.conceptBody:
Body Centered Cubic (BCC) Unit Cell

In a BCC lattice, each corner atom is shared by 8 unit cells while the body centered atom is not shared by any other unit cell. So, the number of effective atoms associated with a BCC unit cell is $\left( \frac{1}{8}\times 8 \right)+1=2$. Thus, the rank of a BCC unit cell is 2.

Thus, $68.20%$ of the volume of the until cell is occupied by atoms and the remaining $31.98%$ is the void volume. This type of structure is exhibited by $\text{K},\text{Fe},\text{Li},\text{V},\text{Cr}$, Mo etc.

concepts.title: Face centered cubic (FCC) unit cell
concepts.conceptBody:
Face Centered Cubic (FCC) Unit Cell

In this structure, two adjacent corner atoms are not $i$ contact with each other and each face centered atom is in contact with four corner atoms of its face. if ‘a’ represents the edge length of a FCC unit cell and ‘ $r$ ‘ is the radius of atom, then face diagonal $(\sqrt{2}a)$ would be equal to 4 r.

Thus, $74.05%$ of the volume of the unit cell is occupied by atoms and the remaining $25-95%$ is the empty space. Al, $\text{Ni},\text{Ag},\text{Fe},\text{Pd},\text{Cu},\text{Au},\text{Pb}$, all solid noble gases etc. exhibit this type of structure.

concepts.title: Coordination number
concepts.conceptBody:
Coordination Number

The number of ions of opposite charge in the immediate surrounding of a given ion is termed its coordination number (C.N.)

C.N. and radius ratio of some crystals are summarised in Table

Limiting Radius Ratios and Structure

concepts.title: Characteristics of Some Typical Crystal Structure
concepts.conceptBody: \(\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \begin{array}{l} \text { Number of } \\ \text { formula units of } \\ \left(\mathrm{AB}, \text { or } \mathrm{AB}_2\right) \text { per } \\ \text { unit cell } \end{array} & \begin{array}{l} \text { Crystal } \\ \text { Type of unit cell } \end{array} & \text { Example } & \mathrm{r}+/ \mathrm{r}- & \mathrm{CN} \\ \hline 1 & \text { Body-centered } & \mathrm{CsCl}, \mathrm{CsBr}, \mathrm{TICl} & 0.93 & 8-8 \\ \hline 4 & \text { Face-centered } & \mathrm{AgCl}, \mathrm{MgO} & 0.52 & 6-6 \\ \hline 4 & \text { Face-centered } & \mathrm{ZnS} & 0.40 & 4-4 \\ \hline 4 & \text { Face-centered } & \begin{array}{l} \text { CaF}_2, \text{SrF}_2, \\ \text { CdF}_2,\text{ ThO}_2 \end{array} & 0.73 & 8-4 \\ \hline \end{array}\)

concepts.title: Density of the Material
concepts.conceptBody:
Unit Cell Dimensions and Density of the Material:

Let number of atoms in the unit cell $=\text{z}$

Edge length$=\text{a}$

Molecular weight or atomic weight of the species $=\text{M}$

Then mass of $\text{z}$ atoms in a unit cell$=\frac{\text{Mz}}{{{\text{N}}_{0}}}$

Volume $={{\text{a}}^{3}}$

Hence density $d=\frac{\text{ mass }}{\text{ volume }}=\frac{\text{Mz}}{{{\text{N}}_{0}}{{\text{a}}^{3}}}$

Note: Volume is ${{\text{a}}^{3}}$ when the unit cell is cubic, it will be different for Hexagonal unit cell.

concepts.title: Calculation of Number of Particles per Unit Cell of a Cubic Crystal System
concepts.conceptBody:
Calculation of Number of Particles per Unit Cell of a Cubic Crystal System

Keeping the following points in mind we can calculate the number of atoms in a unit cell.

  • An atom at the corner is shared by eight unit cells. Hence an atom at the corner contributes $1/8$ to the unit cell
  • An atom at the face is shared by two unit cells

Contribution of each atom on the face is $1/2$ to the unit cell.

  • An atom within the body of the unit cell is shared by no other unit cell

Contribution of each atom within the body is 1 to the unit cell.

  • An atom present on the edge is shared by four unit cells

Contribution of each atom on the edge is $1/4$ to the unit cell.

It can be determined by the relation

$=\frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{c}}}}{8}+\frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{f}}}}{2}+\frac{{{\text{n}}_{\text{i}}}}{1}$

${{\text{n}}_{\text{c}}}=\text{ number of atoms at the corners of the cube }$

${{\text{n}}_{\text{f}}}=\text{ number of atoms at Six faces of cube }$

${{\text{n}}_{\text{i}}}=\text{ number of atoms at do the cube }$

By applying these rules, we can calculate the number of atoms in the different cubic unit cells of monoatomic substances.

concepts.title: Simple Cubic
concepts.conceptBody:
Simple Cubic

In this type of unit cell, there are eight atoms at the corners and each such atom makes $\frac{1}{8}$ contribution to the unit cell.

Hence, a simple cubic unit cell has 8 (at corners) $\frac{1}{8}=1$ atom.

concepts.title: Body Centered Cubic
concepts.conceptBody:
Body Centered Cubic

This type of unit cell has eight atoms at corners and one at the body center. Each corner atom makes $\frac{1}{8}$ contribution and the atom at the body center belongs only to the particular unit cell. Hence, a body centered cubic unit cell has,

$8\text{ (at corners) }\frac{1}{8}+1\text{ (at body center) }\quad 1=2\text{ atoms }$

concepts.title: Face Centered Cubic
concepts.conceptBody:
Face Centered Cubic

A face centered cubic unit cell has one atom at each corner and one atom at each face center, being shared by two unit cells and making a contribution of only $\frac{1}{2}$ to a particular unit cell. Hence, a face cented cubic unit cell has,

$8\text{ (at corners) }\frac{1}{8}+6\text{ (at face centres) }\frac{1}{2}=4\text{ atoms }$

Example 1

An element ‘$X$’ (atomic mass 150) having fec structures has unit cell edge $400\text{pm}$. Calculate the density of ‘$X$’ and the number of unit cell for $15~\text{g}$ of $\text{X}$.

Solution: 

Length of the unit cell edge $=400\text{pm}=400\quad {{10}^{-10}}~\text{cm}$

Volume of unit cell $={{\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 400 & {{10}^{-10}} \\ \end{array} \right)}^{3}}=6.4\quad {{10}^{-23}}~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{3}}$

Since structure is fcc the number of atoms will be 4

Volume of $15~\text{g}$ of $\text{X}=\frac{\text{ Mass }}{\text{ density }}=\frac{15}{15.56}=0.9640\text{cc}$

$\text{No}\text{. of unit cells in }0.9640~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{3}}\text{ }=\frac{\text{ Volume of Substance }}{\text{ Unit cell volume }}$

$=\frac{0.9640}{6.4\quad {{10}^{-23}}}=1.506\quad {{10}^{22}}\text{ unitcells }$

concepts.title: Close packing of spheres in one dimension
concepts.conceptBody: 1. Close packing of spheres in one dimension

concepts.title: Two dimensional close packing
concepts.conceptBody:
2. Two dimensional close packing: 

The rows can be combined in two different ways to form crystal places:

(a) Square close packing -particles when placed in the adjacent rows show a horizontal as well as vertical alignment and form squares.

(b) Hexagonal close packing -Packing in every next row are placed in the depression between the particles of the row. Hexagonal close packing with triangular voids is more efficient.

Spheres are placed in a row forming the crystal edge.

Note: 

(1) Hexagonal close packing is more efficient as more space is accepted by the sphere in this arrangement.

(2) In square packing, a central sphere is in contact with four other spheres, whereas in hexagonal close packing, central sphere is in contact with six other spheres.

concepts.title: Three-dimensional packing
concepts.conceptBody:
3. Three-dimensional packing

Let us now consider a three dimensional packing keeping a hexagonal close packed pattern for layers.

Two types of arrangements are possible.

(a) Hexagonal close packing - AB AB type of arrangement.

(b) Cubic close packing - ABC - ABC type of arrangement.

concepts.title: Hexagonal Close Packing
concepts.conceptBody:
Hexagonal Close Packing

In two-dimensional packing, hexagonal close packing is more efficient. In three dimensions the second layer spheres are placed in the A voids and B voids are unoccupied.

In the second layer there are $\text{C}$ and $\text{D}$ voids.

The third layer is placed such that it covers the $\text{C}$ voids. The third layer spheres are directly over the first layer leading to $\text{AB}AB$ type of arrangement or hexagonal close packing..

concepts.title: Cubic Close Packing
concepts.conceptBody:
Cubic Close Packing

When the third spheres are placed on the D voids a layer different from layers A and B is produced leading to ABC ABC....type arrangement or cubic close packing (CCP) similar to face centered cubic. It is similar to face centered cubic packing.

Example of HCP - Mg, Zn, Cd

Example of CCP - Na, K, Fe, Cr,

In both the above patterns of arrangements the maximum occupied shape is $76%$ of the available volume.

In both HCP and CCP the coordination number is 12 because a sphere is in contact with 6 spheres in its own layer. It touches three spheres in the layer above and three in the layer below:

- Co-ordination number in hcp and ccp

-(i) Hexagonal close packing

-(ii) Cubic close packing

Packing and Structure of Crystals

Packing Fraction and Density of Packing

Let radius of the atom in the packing = r

edge length of the cube$=a$

volume of the cube $V={{a}^{3}}$

volume of the atom (spherical)$\text{v}=\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{r}}^{3}}$

then packing density$=\frac{\text{vz}}{\text{V}}=\frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{r}}^{3}}\text{z}}{{{\text{a}}^{3}}}$

concepts.title: In simple cubic structure
concepts.conceptBody:
I. In simple cubic structure

$\text{r}=\frac{\text{a}}{2}$

$\therefore $ Volume of the atom (spherical)

$=\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{r}}^{3}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\left( \frac{\text{a}}{2} \right)}^{3}}$

$=\frac{\pi {{\text{a}}^{3}}}{6}$

$\therefore $ Volume of the cube $={{\text{a}}^{3}}$

$\therefore $ Packing density

$=\frac{\text{ Volume of the one spherical atom in unit cell }}{\text{ Volume of the cube }}$

$=\frac{\pi {{\text{a}}^{3}}}{6{{\text{a}}^{3}}}=\frac{\pi }{6}=0.5236$

concepts.title: In fcc structure
concepts.conceptBody:
II. In fcc structure [hexagonal closest packed (hep) and cubic closest-packed (ccp)]

$\text{Edge length }=\text{a }$

$\text{face diagonal }=\text{b}=\text{AC}$

Volume of the four spherical atoms of radius $\text{r}$ in the unit cell $=\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{r}}^{3}}\times 4$

$=\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\left( \frac{\text{a}}{2\sqrt{2}} \right)}^{3}}\times 4=\frac{\pi {{\text{a}}^{3}}}{3\sqrt{2}}$

Volume of the cube$={{a}^{3}}$

$\therefore \quad \text{ Packing density }=\frac{\pi {{\text{a}}^{3}}}{3\sqrt{2}\times {{\text{a}}^{3}}}=\frac{\pi }{3\sqrt{2}}=0.7405$

concepts.title: In bcc structure
concepts.conceptBody:
In bcc (body centred cubic) structure

Edge length$=\text{a}$

and radius of the spherical atom in the unit cell $=\text{r}$

Face diagonal $=\text{b}$

and body diagonal $=\text{c}$

Atom at the centre touches other two atoms diagonally arranged

and volume of the cube $={{a}^{3}}$

$\therefore \quad \text{ Packing efficiency }=\frac{\pi \sqrt{3}{{\text{a}}^{3}}}{8{{\text{a}}^{3}}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}\pi }{8}=0.6802$

concepts.title: Summary of the Four Kinds of Packing for Spheres
concepts.conceptBody:
Summary of the Four Kinds of Packing for Spheres

Example 

The length of the unit cell edge of a ‘bce’ lattice metal is $352\text{pm}$. Calculate radius of the atom of the metal.

Solution: 

In a ‘bcc’ structure, the atoms touch each other along the body diagonal.

Let $\quad $ radius of the atom $=R$

then body diagonal $=\text{R}+2\text{R}+\text{R}=4\text{R}=\sqrt{3}\text{a}$

$\therefore \quad \text{R}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\text{a}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\times 352=152.4\text{pm}$

concepts.title: Radius Ratio Rules
concepts.conceptBody:
Radius Ratio Rules

For the stability of an ionic compound, each cation should be surrounded by maximum number of anions and vice verse. The number of oppositely charged ions surrounding each ion is called its co-ordination number.

The larger is the size of the cation the greater is its coordination number. In other words, greater is the radius ratio the greater is its coordination number.

The relationship between the radius and the coordination number and the structural arrangement are called radius ratio rules. The radius ratio rule is given in the table below.

$\text{ Radius ratio }=\left( \frac{\text{ Radius of cation }\left( {{\text{r}}^{+}} \right)}{\text{Radius of anion }\left( {{\text{r}}^{-}} \right)} \right)=\frac{{{\text{r}}^{+}}}{{{\text{r}}^{-}}}$

The above relation may be understood with the following explanation.

For a most stable arrangement the anions must touch each other as well as the cation simultaneously.

For e.g., in a planar triangular structure, the ideal arrangement is represented in the figure.

But if the size of the cation increases (Keeping the size of anions same) then the anions will no longer be touching each others as shown in the figure.

So, readjust each other in such a way that they touch each other as well as the cation. Under such situation four anions may be touching the cation.

The leads to a tetrahedral arrangement.

Similarly in case of an octahedral arrangement, if the size of the cation decrease i.e., the radius ratio decreases the arrangement changes to tetrahedral to become stable. But if the size of the cation increases i.e., the radius ratio increases the arrangement changes to body centred cubic structure to acquire stability.

concepts.title: Radius Ratio for Co-ordination Number 3 (Triangular Arrangement)
concepts.conceptBody:
Radius Ratio for Co-ordination Number 3 (Triangular Arrangement)

The radius ratio for the triangular arrangement of ions can be obtained as follows.

In triangular arrangement (co-ordination number 3), a cation of radius ${{r}_{c}}$ is surrounded by 3 anions of radius ${{r}_{\text{a}}}$. The cation is touching all the three anions and anions are also in contact with each other, an arrangement which gives limiting radius ratio for co-ordination number 3 . From figure it is obvious that

$\text{AB}=\text{BC}=\text{AC}=2{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}$

$\text{AD}={{\text{r}}_{\text{e}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}\text{ and AE}={{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}$

The angle $\text{BAC}$ is 62 and the angle DAE is 30 , then by trigonometry,

$\operatorname{Cos}30=\frac{\text{AE}}{\text{AD}}=\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}$

$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{{{r}_{a}}}{{{r}_{c}}+{{r}_{a}}}$

Taking reciprocal,

$\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}-1=\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}}=0.155$

concepts.title: Radius ratio for co-ordination number 4 (tetrahedral arrangement)
concepts.conceptBody:
Radius ratio for co-ordination number 4 (tetrahedral arrangement)

In the limiting tetrahedral arrangement, a cation of radius,${{r}_{c}}$ is touching all four anions of radius, ${{r}_{a}}$ and the anions are also in contact with each other.

From figure, it is obvious that
$\text{AB}=\text{BC}=\text{CD}=\text{DA}=2{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\text{a}}{\sqrt{2}}$
and $\text{AE}=\text{BE}=\text{CE}=\text{DE}={{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}\text{a}}{4}$
$\therefore \quad \text{ }\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}{2{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}\text{a}}{4}\times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\text{a}}$
$\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}{1{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$
or $\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}+1=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$
$\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}-1=0.225$

concepts.title: Radius ratio for co-ordination number 6 (octahedral arrangement) or radius ratio for co-ordination number 4 (square planar arrangement)
concepts.conceptBody:
Radius ratio for co-ordination number 6 (octahedral arrangement) or radius ratio for co-ordination number 4 (square planar arrangement)

In the limiting octahedral arrangement, a cation of radius ${{r}_{c}}$ is in contact with six anions of radius ${{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}$ and the anions are also in contact with each other.

Draw right angle triangle passing through the centre of the three anions. The cation will lie in the middle of the hypotenuse. From figure, it is obvious that

concepts.title: Radius Ratio for Co-ordination Number 8 (Body Centered Cubic Crystal)
concepts.conceptBody:
Radius Ratio for Co-ordination Number 8 (Body Centered Cubic Crystal)

In the limiting cubical (body centered cubic) arrangement, a cation of radius,${{r}_{c}}$ is touching all the eight anions of radius, ${{r}_{\text{a}}}$ and the anions are also in contact with each other.

Radius ratio rules are used for finding out the type of crystal structure in which ionic solids will crystallise, depending on their co-ordination number. In practice, the radius ratio rules are most reliable when the cation co-ordination number is 8 , less reliable with 6 co-ordinate cations and unreliable for 4 co-ordinate cations.

concepts.title: Tetrahedral Void
concepts.conceptBody:
TYPES OF VOIDS

In closest packing, we have observed two types of voids ‘$a$’ and ‘b’. The voids of ‘a’ type are called tetrahedral voids and voids of ‘$b$’ type are called octahedral voids. These tetrahedral and octahedral voids are present only in FCC and HP unit cells.

Tetrahedral Void

Now let us see the location of tetrahedral void in a FCC lattice. Let us consider a FCC unit cell and assume that corner is the origin of FCC unit cell. We have to find three other atoms with which this corner atom is in contact with. An atom in FCC unit cell touches another atom, if they are present at a distance of $\frac{\text{a}}{\sqrt{2}}$, where a is the edge length of the unit cell. Thus, this corner atom will touch the face centered atom of those faces which will meet at this corner. When the centre of these four atoms are joined, it gives a tetrahedron and the centre of tetrahedron would be the location of tetrahedral void.

The exact location of tetrahedral void in FCC unit cell can also be traced by cutting the FCC unit cell into eight minicubes by three perpendicular cuts. The atoms in the minicube are present at the alternate corners of the minicube and these atoms actually touch one another forming a tetrahedron with a hole (void) in the centre. This tetrahedral void would be the exact centre of minicube. Since, there are eight such minicubes, so there are eight tetrahedral voids in FCC unit cell. This is twice the number of effective atoms in FCC unit cell. Thus, in general the number of effective tetrahedral voids in a unit cell is double the number of effective atoms in that unit cell.

Example 

Compute the percentage void space per unit volume of unit cell in zinc fluoride structure.

Solution: 

Anions occupy fcc positions and half of the tetrahedral holes are occupied by cations. Since there are four anions and 8 tetrahedral holes per unit cell, the fraction of volume occupied by spheres per unit volume of the unit cell is

$=\frac{4\times \left( \frac{4}{3}\pi \text{r}_{\text{a}}^{3} \right)+\frac{1}{2}\times 8\times \left( \frac{4}{3}\pi \text{r}_{\text{c}}^{3} \right)}{16\sqrt{2}\text{r}_{\text{a}}^{3}}=\frac{\pi }{3\sqrt{2}}\left\{ 1+{{\left( \frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}} \right)}^{3}} \right\}$

$\because \quad $ for tetrahedral holes,

$\frac{{{\text{r}}_{\text{c}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{a}}}}=0.225$

$=\frac{\pi }{3\sqrt{2}}\left\{ 1+{{(0.225)}^{3}} \right\}=0.7493$

\[\therefore \] Void volume = 1 – 0.7493

$=0.2507/\text{ unit volume of unit cell}$

$\text{ }%\text{ void space }=25.0%$

concepts.title: Locating Tetrahedral and Octahedral Voids
concepts.conceptBody:
Locating Tetrahedral and Octahedral Voids

The close packed structures have both octahedral and tetrahedral voids. In a ccp structure, there is 1 octahedral void in the centre of the body and 12 octahedral void on the edges. Each one of which is common to four other unit cells. Thus, in cubic close packed structure.

Octahedral voids in centre of cube $=1$

Effective number of octahedral voids located at the 12 edge of $=12\times \frac{1}{4}=3$

$\therefore \quad $ Total number of octahedral voids $=4$

In ccp structure, there are 8 tetrahedral voids. In close packed structure, there are eight spheres in the corners of the unit cell and each sphere is in contact with three groups giving rise to eight tetrahedral voids.

Example 

In a crystalline solid, having formula $\text{A}{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, oxide ions are arranged in cubic close packed lattice while cations $A$ are present in tetrahedral voids and cations $B$ are present in octahedral voids.

  1. What percentage of the tetrahedral voids is occupied by A?

(ii) What percentage of the octahedral voids is occupied by $B$?

Solution: 

In a cubic close packed lattice of oxide ions there would be two tetrahedral voids and one octahedral void for each oxide ion.

$\therefore \quad $ For four oxide ions there would be 8 tetrahedral and four octahedral voids two are occupied by $B$.

Percentage of tetrahedral voids occupied by $A=\frac{1}{8}\times 100=12.5%$

Percentage of tetrahedral voids occupied by $\text{B}=\frac{2}{4}\times 100=50%$
